'福島県南相馬市鹿島区北右田釼宮112', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Mito-jinja, Minamisouma}} [[JA:御刀神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11488802}} == Japanese content == __NOTOC__ {{神社 |名称 = Mito Shrine |画像 = [[File:Haiden and Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|250px|Mito Shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=temporary shrine building|1=UNKNOWN}}]]
Mito Shrine after the earthquake |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094}}{{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757}}112 Kenmiya, Kita-Migita |位置 = {{Coord|37|42|5.5|N|140|59|24.9|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Mito Shrine}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |創建 = era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} |別名 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken-no-miya|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}} |例祭 = {{ill|lt=April 15|Q2497|WD=Q2497}} |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Mito Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Mito Shrine''' (みとじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757}}, {{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old ranking}} was village shrine. It is one of eight Engishiki-listed shrines in Namekata District{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=of Engishiki}}. === Enshrined deity === * Enshrines {{ill|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286}}. ** The Sōma-domain history '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōsōshi|1=UNKNOWN}}' says Susanoo-no-Mikoto, but shrine tradition says this may be a record error confusing the deity of '''Yasaka Shrine''', a precinct shrine of Mito Shrine. === Origin === It is said that in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}}, when {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru}} campaigned eastward, the deity was invited here. In {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325}} 5 ({{ill|lt=905|Q24239|WD=Q24239}}), Mito Shrine was listed among 100 Engishiki shrines of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389}} as a shrine with spiritual efficacy for protection of the state. In {{ill|lt=Kanki|Q1194200|WD=Q1194200}} 1 (1229), the Namekata clan rebuilt shrine buildings and donated sacred fields; in {{ill|lt=Shōtoku|Q1193848|WD=Q1193848|lt=Shōtoku}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1712|Q6871|WD=Q6871}}), lord {{ill|lt=Sōma Takatane|Q8515901|WD=Q8515901}} of {{ill|lt=Sōma Nakamura Domain|Q844079|WD=Q844079}} donated 5 koku 3 shō. It was thus revered by both domain lords and local people. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 11 it was designated village shrine. The enshrined deity is traditionally identified as martial deity Futsunushi-no-Kami of {{ill|lt=Katori Jingū|Q372380|WD=Q372380}} in {{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011}}. As seen in place name "Kenmiya" and shrine name, it is a shrine of the "sword," and records say the {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532}} is an "ancient sword of a thousand years." Standing near the sea amid rice fields, Mito Shrine was hit by the great tsunami of the {{ill|lt=2011|Q1994|WD=Q1994}} {{ill|lt=March 11|Q2398|WD=Q2398}} {{ill|lt=Tohoku earthquake and tsunami|Q36204|WD=Q36204}}. The sacred grove remained, but torii, shrine buildings, and precinct shrines were swept away, and many nearby parishioner homes were also lost. After the disaster, restoration projects were carried out with support from shrine offices of various prefectures; with backing from {{ill|lt=Hokkaidō Shrine Office|Q135249444|WD=Q135249444}} and {{ill|lt=Shitaya Shrine|Q11361339|WD=Q11361339}} in {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490}}, a temporary shrine building was erected in the precinct on June 11. The "image of Susanoo-no-Mikoto," shintai of precinct Yasaka Shrine, was also found in the rubble. === Precinct === * Temporary shrine building (haiden + honden) File:Main approach to Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View from remains of Mito Shrine torii File:Precincts of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine precinct
Yasaka Shrine stands beside the Daijingū stone monument left of shrine building File:Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine building
Objects related to the shrine swept away by tsunami are gathered File:Ruin of Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Site of former Mito Shrine honden File:East view from Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View east from Mito Shrine * Also, west of shrine buildings stand a Daijingū stone monument and a stone-hokora-form Yasaka Shrine (deity: Susanoo-no-Mikoto). File:Yasaka-jinja shrine and Stone shrine of Daijingu.JPG|Precinct shrine of Mito Shrine
Yasaka Shrine and Daijingū stone monument File:Yasaka-jinja shrine at Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Precinct shrine Yasaka Shrine === Mito Shrine before the disaster === File:Haiden of Mito-jinja before Massive earthquake of 11 March,2011.jpg|Mito Shrine haiden before disaster

'福島県南相馬市鹿島区北右田釼宮112', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Mito-jinja, Minamisouma}} [[JA:御刀神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11488802}} == Japanese content == __NOTOC__ {{神社 |名称 = Mito Shrine |画像 = [[File:Haiden and Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|250px|Mito Shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=temporary shrine building|1=UNKNOWN}}]]
Mito Shrine after the earthquake |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094}}{{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757}}112 Kenmiya, Kita-Migita |位置 = {{Coord|37|42|5.5|N|140|59|24.9|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Mito Shrine}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |創建 = era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} |別名 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken-no-miya|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}} |例祭 = {{ill|lt=April 15|Q2497|WD=Q2497}} |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Mito Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Mito Shrine''' (みとじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757}}, {{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old ranking}} was village shrine. It is one of eight Engishiki-listed shrines in Namekata District{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=of Engishiki}}. === Enshrined deity === * Enshrines {{ill|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286}}. ** The Sōma-domain history '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōsōshi|1=UNKNOWN}}' says Susanoo-no-Mikoto, but shrine tradition says this may be a record error confusing the deity of '''Yasaka Shrine''', a precinct shrine of Mito Shrine. === Origin === It is said that in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}}, when {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru}} campaigned eastward, the deity was invited here. In {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325}} 5 ({{ill|lt=905|Q24239|WD=Q24239}}), Mito Shrine was listed among 100 Engishiki shrines of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389}} as a shrine with spiritual efficacy for protection of the state. In {{ill|lt=Kanki|Q1194200|WD=Q1194200}} 1 (1229), the Namekata clan rebuilt shrine buildings and donated sacred fields; in {{ill|lt=Shōtoku|Q1193848|WD=Q1193848|lt=Shōtoku}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1712|Q6871|WD=Q6871}}), lord {{ill|lt=Sōma Takatane|Q8515901|WD=Q8515901}} of {{ill|lt=Sōma Nakamura Domain|Q844079|WD=Q844079}} donated 5 koku 3 shō. It was thus revered by both domain lords and local people. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 11 it was designated village shrine. The enshrined deity is traditionally identified as martial deity Futsunushi-no-Kami of {{ill|lt=Katori Jingū|Q372380|WD=Q372380}} in {{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011}}. As seen in place name "Kenmiya" and shrine name, it is a shrine of the "sword," and records say the {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532}} is an "ancient sword of a thousand years." Standing near the sea amid rice fields, Mito Shrine was hit by the great tsunami of the {{ill|lt=2011|Q1994|WD=Q1994}} {{ill|lt=March 11|Q2398|WD=Q2398}} {{ill|lt=Tohoku earthquake and tsunami|Q36204|WD=Q36204}}. The sacred grove remained, but torii, shrine buildings, and precinct shrines were swept away, and many nearby parishioner homes were also lost. After the disaster, restoration projects were carried out with support from shrine offices of various prefectures; with backing from {{ill|lt=Hokkaidō Shrine Office|Q135249444|WD=Q135249444}} and {{ill|lt=Shitaya Shrine|Q11361339|WD=Q11361339}} in {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490}}, a temporary shrine building was erected in the precinct on June 11. The "image of Susanoo-no-Mikoto," shintai of precinct Yasaka Shrine, was also found in the rubble. === Precinct === * Temporary shrine building (haiden + honden) File:Main approach to Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View from remains of Mito Shrine torii File:Precincts of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine precinct
Yasaka Shrine stands beside the Daijingū stone monument left of shrine building File:Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine building
Objects related to the shrine swept away by tsunami are gathered File:Ruin of Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Site of former Mito Shrine honden File:East view from Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View east from Mito Shrine * Also, west of shrine buildings stand a Daijingū stone monument and a stone-hokora-form Yasaka Shrine (deity: Susanoo-no-Mikoto). File:Yasaka-jinja shrine and Stone shrine of Daijingu.JPG|Precinct shrine of Mito Shrine
Yasaka Shrine and Daijingū stone monument File:Yasaka-jinja shrine at Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Precinct shrine Yasaka Shrine === Mito Shrine before the disaster === File:Haiden of Mito-jinja before Massive earthquake of 11 March,2011.jpg|Mito Shrine haiden before disaster

'福島県南相馬市鹿島区北右田釼宮112', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Mito-jinja, Minamisouma}} [[JA:御刀神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11488802}} == Japanese content == __NOTOC__ {{神社 |名称 = Mito Shrine |画像 = [[File:Haiden and Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|250px|Mito Shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=temporary shrine building|1=UNKNOWN}}]]
Mito Shrine after the earthquake |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094}}{{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757}}112 Kenmiya, Kita-Migita |位置 = {{Coord|37|42|5.5|N|140|59|24.9|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Mito Shrine}} |祭神 = {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |創建 = era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} |別名 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken-no-miya|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}} |例祭 = {{ill|lt=April 15|Q2497|WD=Q2497}} |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Mito Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Mito Shrine''' (みとじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757}}, {{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old ranking}} was village shrine. It is one of eight Engishiki-listed shrines in Namekata District{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=of Engishiki}}. === Enshrined deity === * Enshrines {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286}}. ** The Sōma-domain history '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōsōshi|1=UNKNOWN}}' says Susanoo-no-Mikoto, but shrine tradition says this may be a record error confusing the deity of '''Yasaka Shrine''', a precinct shrine of Mito Shrine. === Origin === It is said that in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}}, when {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru}} campaigned eastward, the deity was invited here. In {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325}} 5 ({{ill|lt=905|Q24239|WD=Q24239}}), Mito Shrine was listed among 100 Engishiki shrines of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389}} as a shrine with spiritual efficacy for protection of the state. In {{ill|lt=Kanki|Q1194200|WD=Q1194200}} 1 (1229), the Namekata clan rebuilt shrine buildings and donated sacred fields; in {{ill|lt=Shōtoku|Q1193848|WD=Q1193848|lt=Shōtoku}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1712|Q6871|WD=Q6871}}), lord {{ill|lt=Sōma Takatane|Q8515901|WD=Q8515901}} of {{ill|lt=Sōma Nakamura Domain|Q844079|WD=Q844079}} donated 5 koku 3 shō. It was thus revered by both domain lords and local people. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 11 it was designated village shrine. The enshrined deity is traditionally identified as martial deity Futsunushi-no-Kami of {{ill|lt=Katori Jingū|Q372380|WD=Q372380}} in {{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011}}. As seen in place name "Kenmiya" and shrine name, it is a shrine of the "sword," and records say the {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532}} is an "ancient sword of a thousand years." Standing near the sea amid rice fields, Mito Shrine was hit by the great tsunami of the {{ill|lt=2011|Q1994|WD=Q1994}} {{ill|lt=March 11|Q2398|WD=Q2398}} {{ill|lt=Tohoku earthquake and tsunami|Q36204|WD=Q36204}}. The sacred grove remained, but torii, shrine buildings, and precinct shrines were swept away, and many nearby parishioner homes were also lost. After the disaster, restoration projects were carried out with support from shrine offices of various prefectures; with backing from {{ill|lt=Hokkaidō Shrine Office|Q135249444|WD=Q135249444}} and {{ill|lt=Shitaya Shrine|Q11361339|WD=Q11361339}} in {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490}}, a temporary shrine building was erected in the precinct on June 11. The "image of Susanoo-no-Mikoto," shintai of precinct Yasaka Shrine, was also found in the rubble. === Precinct === * Temporary shrine building (haiden + honden) File:Main approach to Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View from remains of Mito Shrine torii File:Precincts of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine precinct
Yasaka Shrine stands beside the Daijingū stone monument left of shrine building File:Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine building
Objects related to the shrine swept away by tsunami are gathered File:Ruin of Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Site of former Mito Shrine honden File:East view from Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View east from Mito Shrine * Also, west of shrine buildings stand a Daijingū stone monument and a stone-hokora-form Yasaka Shrine (deity: Susanoo-no-Mikoto). File:Yasaka-jinja shrine and Stone shrine of Daijingu.JPG|Precinct shrine of Mito Shrine
Yasaka Shrine and Daijingū stone monument File:Yasaka-jinja shrine at Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Precinct shrine Yasaka Shrine === Mito Shrine before the disaster === File:Haiden of Mito-jinja before Massive earthquake of 11 March,2011.jpg|Mito Shrine haiden before disaster

'福島県南相馬市鹿島区北右田釼宮112', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Mito-jinja, Minamisouma}} [[JA:御刀神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11488802}} == Japanese content == __NOTOC__ {{神社 |名称 = Mito Shrine |画像 = [[File:Haiden and Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|250px|Mito Shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=temporary shrine building|1=UNKNOWN}}]]
Mito Shrine after the earthquake |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094}}{{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757}}112 Kenmiya, Kita-Migita |位置 = {{Coord|37|42|5.5|N|140|59|24.9|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Mito Shrine}} |祭神 = {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |創建 = era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} |別名 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken-no-miya|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}} |例祭 = {{ill|lt=April 15|Q2497|WD=Q2497}} |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Mito Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Mito Shrine''' (みとじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757}}, {{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old ranking}} was village shrine. It is one of eight Engishiki-listed shrines in Namekata District{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=of Engishiki}}. === Enshrined deity === * Enshrines {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286}}. ** The Sōma-domain history '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōsōshi|1=UNKNOWN}}' says Susanoo-no-Mikoto, but shrine tradition says this may be a record error confusing the deity of '''Yasaka Shrine''', a precinct shrine of Mito Shrine. === Origin === It is said that in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}}, when {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru}} campaigned eastward, the deity was invited here. In {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325}} 5 ({{ill|lt=905|Q24239|WD=Q24239}}), Mito Shrine was listed among 100 Engishiki shrines of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389}} as a shrine with spiritual efficacy for protection of the state. In {{ill|lt=Kanki|Q1194200|WD=Q1194200}} 1 (1229), the Namekata clan rebuilt shrine buildings and donated sacred fields; in {{ill|lt=Shōtoku|Q1193848|WD=Q1193848|lt=Shōtoku}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1712|Q6871|WD=Q6871}}), lord {{ill|lt=Sōma Takatane|Q8515901|WD=Q8515901}} of {{ill|lt=Sōma Nakamura Domain|Q844079|WD=Q844079}} donated 5 koku 3 shō. It was thus revered by both domain lords and local people. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 11 it was designated village shrine. The enshrined deity is traditionally identified as martial deity Futsunushi-no-Kami of {{ill|lt=Katori Jingū|Q372380|WD=Q372380}} in {{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011}}. As seen in place name "Kenmiya" and shrine name, it is a shrine of the "sword," and records say the {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532}} is an "ancient sword of a thousand years." Standing near the sea amid rice fields, Mito Shrine was hit by the great tsunami of the {{ill|lt=2011|Q1994|WD=Q1994}} {{ill|lt=March 11|Q2398|WD=Q2398}} {{ill|lt=Tohoku earthquake and tsunami|Q36204|WD=Q36204}}. The sacred grove remained, but torii, shrine buildings, and precinct shrines were swept away, and many nearby parishioner homes were also lost. After the disaster, restoration projects were carried out with support from shrine offices of various prefectures; with backing from {{ill|lt=Hokkaidō Shrine Office|Q135249444|WD=Q135249444}} and {{ill|lt=Shitaya Shrine|Q11361339|WD=Q11361339}} in {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490}}, a temporary shrine building was erected in the precinct on June 11. The "image of Susanoo-no-Mikoto," shintai of precinct Yasaka Shrine, was also found in the rubble. === Precinct === * Temporary shrine building (haiden + honden) File:Main approach to Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View from remains of Mito Shrine torii File:Precincts of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine precinct
Yasaka Shrine stands beside the Daijingū stone monument left of shrine building File:Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine building
Objects related to the shrine swept away by tsunami are gathered File:Ruin of Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Site of former Mito Shrine honden File:East view from Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View east from Mito Shrine * Also, west of shrine buildings stand a Daijingū stone monument and a stone-hokora-form Yasaka Shrine (deity: Susanoo-no-Mikoto). File:Yasaka-jinja shrine and Stone shrine of Daijingu.JPG|Precinct shrine of Mito Shrine
Yasaka Shrine and Daijingū stone monument File:Yasaka-jinja shrine at Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Precinct shrine Yasaka Shrine === Mito Shrine before the disaster === File:Haiden of Mito-jinja before Massive earthquake of 11 March,2011.jpg|Mito Shrine haiden before disaster
Provided by Mr. Mori Yukihiko, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ise Ōmikami Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Image of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, shintai of precinct Yasaka Shrine
Provided by Mr. Mori Yukihiko, Ise Ōmikami Shrine === References === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōsō|1=UNKNOWN}}shi (Sōma City History 4), {{ill|lt=Sōma|Q696456|WD=Q696456}}, 1969 {{Reflist}} === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related item === {{Commonscat}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=福島県}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みとしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Mito Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Mito Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:福島県の神社]] [[Category:福島県の旧村社]] [[Category:陸奥国の式内社]] [[Category:行方郡 (福島県)|社みとしんしや]] [[Category:南相馬市の建築物]] [[Category:南相馬市の歴史]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

[[File:Haiden and Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|250px|Mito Shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=temporary shrine building|1=UNKNOWN}}]]
Mito Shrine after the earthquake |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094|1=Minamisōma}}{{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757|1=Kashima-ku}}112 Kenmiya, Kita-Migita |位置 = {{Coord|37|42|5.5|N|140|59|24.9|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Mito Shrine}} |祭神 = {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} |別名 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken-no-miya|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}} |例祭 = {{ill|lt=April 15|Q2497|WD=Q2497|1=April 15}} |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Mito Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Mito Shrine''' (みとじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757|1=Kashima-ku}}, {{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094|1=Minamisōma}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old ranking|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was village shrine. It is one of eight Engishiki-listed shrines in Namekata District{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=of Engishiki|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. === Enshrined deity === * Enshrines {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}}. ** The Sōma-domain history '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōsōshi|1=UNKNOWN}}' says Susanoo-no-Mikoto, but shrine tradition says this may be a record error confusing the deity of '''Yasaka Shrine''', a precinct shrine of Mito Shrine. === Origin === It is said that in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}, when {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} campaigned eastward, the deity was invited here. In {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}} 5 ({{ill|lt=905|Q24239|WD=Q24239|1=905}}), Mito Shrine was listed among 100 Engishiki shrines of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}} as a shrine with spiritual efficacy for protection of the state. In {{ill|lt=Kanki|Q1194200|WD=Q1194200|1=Kangi}} 1 (1229), the Namekata clan rebuilt shrine buildings and donated sacred fields; in {{ill|lt=Shōtoku|Q1193848|WD=Q1193848|lt=Shōtoku|1=Shōtoku (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1712|Q6871|WD=Q6871|1=1712}}), lord {{ill|lt=Sōma Takatane|Q8515901|WD=Q8515901|1=Sōma Takatane}} of {{ill|lt=Sōma Nakamura Domain|Q844079|WD=Q844079|1=Sōma Nakamura Domain}} donated 5 koku 3 shō. It was thus revered by both domain lords and local people. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 11 it was designated village shrine. The enshrined deity is traditionally identified as martial deity Futsunushi-no-Kami of {{ill|lt=Katori Jingū|Q372380|WD=Q372380|1=Katori Jingū}} in {{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011|1=Chiba Prefecture}}. As seen in place name "Kenmiya" and shrine name, it is a shrine of the "sword," and records say the {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532|1=Shintai}} is an "ancient sword of a thousand years." Standing near the sea amid rice fields, Mito Shrine was hit by the great tsunami of the {{ill|lt=2011|Q1994|WD=Q1994|1=2011}} {{ill|lt=March 11|Q2398|WD=Q2398|1=March 11}} {{ill|lt=Tohoku earthquake and tsunami|Q36204|WD=Q36204|1=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}. The sacred grove remained, but torii, shrine buildings, and precinct shrines were swept away, and many nearby parishioner homes were also lost. After the disaster, restoration projects were carried out with support from shrine offices of various prefectures; with backing from {{ill|lt=Hokkaidō Shrine Office|Q135249444|WD=Q135249444|1=Hokkaido Prefectural Shrine Association}} and {{ill|lt=Shitaya Shrine|Q11361339|WD=Q11361339|1=Shitaya Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}, a temporary shrine building was erected in the precinct on June 11. The "image of Susanoo-no-Mikoto," shintai of precinct Yasaka Shrine, was also found in the rubble. === Precinct === * Temporary shrine building (haiden + honden) File:Main approach to Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View from remains of Mito Shrine torii File:Precincts of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine precinct
Yasaka Shrine stands beside the Daijingū stone monument left of shrine building File:Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine building
Objects related to the shrine swept away by tsunami are gathered File:Ruin of Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Site of former Mito Shrine honden File:East view from Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View east from Mito Shrine * Also, west of shrine buildings stand a Daijingū stone monument and a stone-hokora-form Yasaka Shrine (deity: Susanoo-no-Mikoto). File:Yasaka-jinja shrine and Stone shrine of Daijingu.JPG|Precinct shrine of Mito Shrine
Yasaka Shrine and Daijingū stone monument File:Yasaka-jinja shrine at Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Precinct shrine Yasaka Shrine === Mito Shrine before the disaster === File:Haiden of Mito-jinja before Massive earthquake of 11 March,2011.jpg|Mito Shrine haiden before disaster

'福島県南相馬市鹿島区北右田釼宮112', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Mito-jinja, Minamisouma}} [[JA:御刀神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11488802}} == Japanese content == __NOTOC__ {{神社 |名称 = Mito Shrine |画像 = [[File:Haiden and Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|250px|Mito Shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=temporary shrine building|1=UNKNOWN}}]]
Mito Shrine after the earthquake |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094|1=Minamisōma}}{{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757|1=Kashima-ku}}112 Kenmiya, Kita-Migita |位置 = {{Coord|37|42|5.5|N|140|59|24.9|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Mito Shrine}} |祭神 = {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} |別名 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken-no-miya|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}} |例祭 = {{ill|lt=April 15|Q2497|WD=Q2497|1=April 15}} |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Mito Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Mito Shrine''' (みとじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757|1=Kashima-ku}}, {{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094|1=Minamisōma}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old ranking|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was village shrine. It is one of eight Engishiki-listed shrines in Namekata District{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=of Engishiki|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. === Enshrined deity === * Enshrines {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}}. ** The Sōma-domain history '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōsōshi|1=UNKNOWN}}' says Susanoo-no-Mikoto, but shrine tradition says this may be a record error confusing the deity of '''Yasaka Shrine''', a precinct shrine of Mito Shrine. === Origin === It is said that in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}, when {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} campaigned eastward, the deity was invited here. In {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}} 5 ({{ill|lt=905|Q24239|WD=Q24239|1=905}}), Mito Shrine was listed among 100 Engishiki shrines of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}} as a shrine with spiritual efficacy for protection of the state. In {{ill|lt=Kanki|Q1194200|WD=Q1194200|1=Kangi}} 1 (1229), the Namekata clan rebuilt shrine buildings and donated sacred fields; in {{ill|lt=Shōtoku|Q1193848|WD=Q1193848|lt=Shōtoku|1=Shōtoku (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1712|Q6871|WD=Q6871|1=1712}}), lord {{ill|lt=Sōma Takatane|Q8515901|WD=Q8515901|1=Sōma Takatane}} of {{ill|lt=Sōma Nakamura Domain|Q844079|WD=Q844079|1=Sōma Nakamura Domain}} donated 5 koku 3 shō. It was thus revered by both domain lords and local people. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 11 it was designated village shrine. The enshrined deity is traditionally identified as martial deity Futsunushi-no-Kami of {{ill|lt=Katori Jingū|Q372380|WD=Q372380|1=Katori Jingū}} in {{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011|1=Chiba Prefecture}}. As seen in place name "Kenmiya" and shrine name, it is a shrine of the "sword," and records say the {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532|1=Shintai}} is an "ancient sword of a thousand years." Standing near the sea amid rice fields, Mito Shrine was hit by the great tsunami of the {{ill|lt=2011|Q1994|WD=Q1994|1=2011}} {{ill|lt=March 11|Q2398|WD=Q2398|1=March 11}} {{ill|lt=Tohoku earthquake and tsunami|Q36204|WD=Q36204|1=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}. The sacred grove remained, but torii, shrine buildings, and precinct shrines were swept away, and many nearby parishioner homes were also lost. After the disaster, restoration projects were carried out with support from shrine offices of various prefectures; with backing from {{ill|lt=Hokkaidō Shrine Office|Q135249444|WD=Q135249444|1=Hokkaido Prefectural Shrine Association}} and {{ill|lt=Shitaya Shrine|Q11361339|WD=Q11361339|1=Shitaya Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}, a temporary shrine building was erected in the precinct on June 11. The "image of Susanoo-no-Mikoto," shintai of precinct Yasaka Shrine, was also found in the rubble. === Precinct === * Temporary shrine building (haiden + honden) File:Main approach to Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View from remains of Mito Shrine torii File:Precincts of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine precinct
Yasaka Shrine stands beside the Daijingū stone monument left of shrine building File:Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine building
Objects related to the shrine swept away by tsunami are gathered File:Ruin of Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Site of former Mito Shrine honden File:East view from Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View east from Mito Shrine * Also, west of shrine buildings stand a Daijingū stone monument and a stone-hokora-form Yasaka Shrine (deity: Susanoo-no-Mikoto). File:Yasaka-jinja shrine and Stone shrine of Daijingu.JPG|Precinct shrine of Mito Shrine
Yasaka Shrine and Daijingū stone monument File:Yasaka-jinja shrine at Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Precinct shrine Yasaka Shrine === Mito Shrine before the disaster === File:Haiden of Mito-jinja before Massive earthquake of 11 March,2011.jpg|Mito Shrine haiden before disaster
Provided by Mr. Mori Yukihiko, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ise Ōmikami Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Image of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, shintai of precinct Yasaka Shrine

== name in kana (P1814) == * みとじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 6380005007220 == Commons category (P373) == * Mito-jinja, Minamisouma == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '福島県南相馬市鹿島区北右田釼宮112', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Mito-jinja, Minamisouma}} [[JA:御刀神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11488802}} == Japanese content == __NOTOC__ {{神社 |名称 = Mito Shrine |画像 = [[File:Haiden and Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|250px|Mito Shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=temporary shrine building|1=UNKNOWN}}]]
Mito Shrine after the earthquake |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094|1=Minamisōma}}{{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757|1=Kashima-ku}}112 Kenmiya, Kita-Migita |位置 = {{Coord|37|42|5.5|N|140|59|24.9|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Mito Shrine}} |祭神 = {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} |別名 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken-no-miya|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}} |例祭 = {{ill|lt=April 15|Q2497|WD=Q2497|1=April 15}} |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Mito Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Mito Shrine''' (みとじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757|1=Kashima-ku}}, {{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094|1=Minamisōma}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old ranking|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was village shrine. It is one of eight Engishiki-listed shrines in Namekata District{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=of Engishiki|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. === Enshrined deity === * Enshrines {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}}. ** The Sōma-domain history '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōsōshi|1=UNKNOWN}}' says Susanoo-no-Mikoto, but shrine tradition says this may be a record error confusing the deity of '''Yasaka Shrine''', a precinct shrine of Mito Shrine. === Origin === It is said that in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}, when {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} campaigned eastward, the deity was invited here. In {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}} 5 ({{ill|lt=905|Q24239|WD=Q24239|1=905}}), Mito Shrine was listed among 100 Engishiki shrines of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}} as a shrine with spiritual efficacy for protection of the state. In {{ill|lt=Kanki|Q1194200|WD=Q1194200|1=Kangi}} 1 (1229), the Namekata clan rebuilt shrine buildings and donated sacred fields; in {{ill|lt=Shōtoku|Q1193848|WD=Q1193848|lt=Shōtoku|1=Shōtoku (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1712|Q6871|WD=Q6871|1=1712}}), lord {{ill|lt=Sōma Takatane|Q8515901|WD=Q8515901|1=Sōma Takatane}} of {{ill|lt=Sōma Nakamura Domain|Q844079|WD=Q844079|1=Sōma Nakamura Domain}} donated 5 koku 3 shō. It was thus revered by both domain lords and local people. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 11 it was designated village shrine. The enshrined deity is traditionally identified as martial deity Futsunushi-no-Kami of {{ill|lt=Katori Jingū|Q372380|WD=Q372380|1=Katori Jingū}} in {{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011|1=Chiba Prefecture}}. As seen in place name "Kenmiya" and shrine name, it is a shrine of the "sword," and records say the {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532|1=Shintai}} is an "ancient sword of a thousand years." Standing near the sea amid rice fields, Mito Shrine was hit by the great tsunami of the {{ill|lt=2011|Q1994|WD=Q1994|1=2011}} {{ill|lt=March 11|Q2398|WD=Q2398|1=March 11}} {{ill|lt=Tohoku earthquake and tsunami|Q36204|WD=Q36204|1=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}. The sacred grove remained, but torii, shrine buildings, and precinct shrines were swept away, and many nearby parishioner homes were also lost. After the disaster, restoration projects were carried out with support from shrine offices of various prefectures; with backing from {{ill|lt=Hokkaidō Shrine Office|Q135249444|WD=Q135249444|1=Hokkaido Prefectural Shrine Association}} and {{ill|lt=Shitaya Shrine|Q11361339|WD=Q11361339|1=Shitaya Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}, a temporary shrine building was erected in the precinct on June 11. The "image of Susanoo-no-Mikoto," shintai of precinct Yasaka Shrine, was also found in the rubble. === Precinct === * Temporary shrine building (haiden + honden) File:Main approach to Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View from remains of Mito Shrine torii File:Precincts of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine precinct
Yasaka Shrine stands beside the Daijingū stone monument left of shrine building File:Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine building
Objects related to the shrine swept away by tsunami are gathered File:Ruin of Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Site of former Mito Shrine honden File:East view from Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View east from Mito Shrine * Also, west of shrine buildings stand a Daijingū stone monument and a stone-hokora-form Yasaka Shrine (deity: Susanoo-no-Mikoto). File:Yasaka-jinja shrine and Stone shrine of Daijingu.JPG|Precinct shrine of Mito Shrine
Yasaka Shrine and Daijingū stone monument File:Yasaka-jinja shrine at Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Precinct shrine Yasaka Shrine === Mito Shrine before the disaster === File:Haiden of Mito-jinja before Massive earthquake of 11 March,2011.jpg|Mito Shrine haiden before disaster
Provided by Mr. Mori Yukihiko, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ise Ōmikami Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Image of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, shintai of precinct Yasaka Shrine
Provided by Mr. Mori Yukihiko, Ise Ōmikami Shrine === References === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōsō|1=UNKNOWN}}shi (Sōma City History 4), {{ill|lt=Sōma|Q696456|WD=Q696456|1=Sōma, Fukushima}}, 1969 {{Reflist}} === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related item === {{Commonscat}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=福島県}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みとしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Mito Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Mito Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:福島県の神社]] [[Category:福島県の旧村社]] [[Category:陸奥国の式内社]] [[Category:行方郡 (福島県)|社みとしんしや]] [[Category:南相馬市の建築物]] [[Category:南相馬市の歴史]][[Category:qq]]

== name in kana (P1814) == * みとじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 6380005007220 == Commons category (P373) == * Mito-jinja, Minamisouma == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '福島県南相馬市鹿島区北右田釼宮112', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Mito-jinja, Minamisouma}} [[JA:御刀神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11488802}} == Japanese content == __NOTOC__ {{神社 |名称 = Mito Shrine |画像 = [[File:Haiden and Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|250px|Mito Shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=temporary shrine building|1=UNKNOWN}}]]
Mito Shrine after the earthquake |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094|1=Minamisōma}}{{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757|1=Kashima-ku}}112 Kenmiya, Kita-Migita |位置 = {{Coord|37|42|5.5|N|140|59|24.9|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Mito Shrine}} |祭神 = {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} |別名 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken-no-miya|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}} |例祭 = {{ill|lt=April 15|Q2497|WD=Q2497|1=April 15}} |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Mito Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Mito Shrine''' (みとじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757|1=Kashima-ku}}, {{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094|1=Minamisōma}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old ranking|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was village shrine. It is one of eight Engishiki-listed shrines in Namekata District{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=of Engishiki|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. === Enshrined deity === * Enshrines {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}}. ** The Sōma-domain history '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōsōshi|1=UNKNOWN}}' says Susanoo-no-Mikoto, but shrine tradition says this may be a record error confusing the deity of '''Yasaka Shrine''', a precinct shrine of Mito Shrine. === Origin === It is said that in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}, when {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} campaigned eastward, the deity was invited here. In {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}} 5 ({{ill|lt=905|Q24239|WD=Q24239|1=905}}), Mito Shrine was listed among 100 Engishiki shrines of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}} as a shrine with spiritual efficacy for protection of the state. In {{ill|lt=Kanki|Q1194200|WD=Q1194200|1=Kangi}} 1 (1229), the Namekata clan rebuilt shrine buildings and donated sacred fields; in {{ill|lt=Shōtoku|Q1193848|WD=Q1193848|lt=Shōtoku|1=Shōtoku (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1712|Q6871|WD=Q6871|1=1712}}), lord {{ill|lt=Sōma Takatane|Q8515901|WD=Q8515901|1=Sōma Takatane}} of {{ill|lt=Sōma Nakamura Domain|Q844079|WD=Q844079|1=Sōma Nakamura Domain}} donated 5 koku 3 shō. It was thus revered by both domain lords and local people. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 11 it was designated village shrine. The enshrined deity is traditionally identified as martial deity Futsunushi-no-Kami of {{ill|lt=Katori Jingū|Q372380|WD=Q372380|1=Katori Jingū}} in {{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011|1=Chiba Prefecture}}. As seen in place name "Kenmiya" and shrine name, it is a shrine of the "sword," and records say the {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532|1=Shintai}} is an "ancient sword of a thousand years." Standing near the sea amid rice fields, Mito Shrine was hit by the great tsunami of the {{ill|lt=2011|Q1994|WD=Q1994|1=2011}} {{ill|lt=March 11|Q2398|WD=Q2398|1=March 11}} {{ill|lt=Tohoku earthquake and tsunami|Q36204|WD=Q36204|1=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}. The sacred grove remained, but torii, shrine buildings, and precinct shrines were swept away, and many nearby parishioner homes were also lost. After the disaster, restoration projects were carried out with support from shrine offices of various prefectures; with backing from {{ill|lt=Hokkaidō Shrine Office|Q135249444|WD=Q135249444|1=Hokkaido Prefectural Shrine Association}} and {{ill|lt=Shitaya Shrine|Q11361339|WD=Q11361339|1=Shitaya Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}, a temporary shrine building was erected in the precinct on June 11. The "image of Susanoo-no-Mikoto," shintai of precinct Yasaka Shrine, was also found in the rubble. === Precinct === * Temporary shrine building (haiden + honden) File:Main approach to Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View from remains of Mito Shrine torii File:Precincts of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine precinct
Yasaka Shrine stands beside the Daijingū stone monument left of shrine building File:Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine building
Objects related to the shrine swept away by tsunami are gathered File:Ruin of Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Site of former Mito Shrine honden File:East view from Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View east from Mito Shrine * Also, west of shrine buildings stand a Daijingū stone monument and a stone-hokora-form Yasaka Shrine (deity: Susanoo-no-Mikoto). File:Yasaka-jinja shrine and Stone shrine of Daijingu.JPG|Precinct shrine of Mito Shrine
Yasaka Shrine and Daijingū stone monument File:Yasaka-jinja shrine at Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Precinct shrine Yasaka Shrine === Mito Shrine before the disaster === File:Haiden of Mito-jinja before Massive earthquake of 11 March,2011.jpg|Mito Shrine haiden before disaster
Provided by Mr. Mori Yukihiko, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ise Ōmikami Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Image of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, shintai of precinct Yasaka Shrine
Provided by Mr. Mori Yukihiko, Ise Ōmikami Shrine === References === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōsō|1=UNKNOWN}}shi (Sōma City History 4), {{ill|lt=Sōma|Q696456|WD=Q696456|1=Sōma, Fukushima}}, 1969 {{Reflist}} === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related item === {{Commonscat}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=福島県}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Mito Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Mito Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:福島県の神社]] [[Category:福島県の旧村社]] [[Category:陸奥国の式内社]] [[Category:行方郡 (福島県)|社みとしんしや]] [[Category:南相馬市の建築物]] [[Category:南相馬市の歴史]][[Category:qq]]

== name in kana (P1814) == * みとじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 6380005007220 == Commons category (P373) == * Mito-jinja, Minamisouma == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '福島県南相馬市鹿島区北右田釼宮112', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Mito-jinja, Minamisouma}} [[JA:御刀神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11488802}} == Japanese content == __NOTOC__ {{神社 |名称 = Mito Shrine |画像 = [[File:Haiden and Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|250px|Mito Shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=temporary shrine building|1=UNKNOWN}}]]
Mito Shrine after the earthquake |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094|1=Minamisōma}}{{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757|1=Kashima-ku}}112 Kenmiya, Kita-Migita |位置 = {{Coord|37|42|5.5|N|140|59|24.9|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Mito Shrine}} |祭神 = {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} |別名 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken-no-miya|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}} |例祭 = {{ill|lt=April 15|Q2497|WD=Q2497|1=April 15}} |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Mito Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Mito Shrine''' (みとじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757|1=Kashima-ku}}, {{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094|1=Minamisōma}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old ranking|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was village shrine. It is one of eight Engishiki-listed shrines in Namekata District{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=of Engishiki|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. === Enshrined deity === * Enshrines {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}}. ** The Sōma-domain history '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōsōshi|1=UNKNOWN}}' says Susanoo-no-Mikoto, but shrine tradition says this may be a record error confusing the deity of '''Yasaka Shrine''', a precinct shrine of Mito Shrine. === Origin === It is said that in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}, when {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} campaigned eastward, the deity was invited here. In {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}} 5 ({{ill|lt=905|Q24239|WD=Q24239|1=905}}), Mito Shrine was listed among 100 Engishiki shrines of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}} as a shrine with spiritual efficacy for protection of the state. In {{ill|lt=Kanki|Q1194200|WD=Q1194200|1=Kangi}} 1 (1229), the Namekata clan rebuilt shrine buildings and donated sacred fields; in {{ill|lt=Shōtoku|Q1193848|WD=Q1193848|lt=Shōtoku|1=Shōtoku (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1712|Q6871|WD=Q6871|1=1712}}), lord {{ill|lt=Sōma Takatane|Q8515901|WD=Q8515901|1=Sōma Takatane}} of {{ill|lt=Sōma Nakamura Domain|Q844079|WD=Q844079|1=Sōma Nakamura Domain}} donated 5 koku 3 shō. It was thus revered by both domain lords and local people. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 11 it was designated village shrine. The enshrined deity is traditionally identified as martial deity Futsunushi-no-Kami of {{ill|lt=Katori Jingū|Q372380|WD=Q372380|1=Katori Jingū}} in {{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011|1=Chiba Prefecture}}. As seen in place name "Kenmiya" and shrine name, it is a shrine of the "sword," and records say the {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532|1=Shintai}} is an "ancient sword of a thousand years." Standing near the sea amid rice fields, Mito Shrine was hit by the great tsunami of the {{ill|lt=2011|Q1994|WD=Q1994|1=2011}} {{ill|lt=March 11|Q2398|WD=Q2398|1=March 11}} {{ill|lt=Tohoku earthquake and tsunami|Q36204|WD=Q36204|1=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}. The sacred grove remained, but torii, shrine buildings, and precinct shrines were swept away, and many nearby parishioner homes were also lost. After the disaster, restoration projects were carried out with support from shrine offices of various prefectures; with backing from {{ill|lt=Hokkaidō Shrine Office|Q135249444|WD=Q135249444|1=Hokkaido Prefectural Shrine Association}} and {{ill|lt=Shitaya Shrine|Q11361339|WD=Q11361339|1=Shitaya Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}, a temporary shrine building was erected in the precinct on June 11. The "image of Susanoo-no-Mikoto," shintai of precinct Yasaka Shrine, was also found in the rubble. === Precinct === * Temporary shrine building (haiden + honden) File:Main approach to Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View from remains of Mito Shrine torii File:Precincts of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine precinct
Yasaka Shrine stands beside the Daijingū stone monument left of shrine building File:Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine building
Objects related to the shrine swept away by tsunami are gathered File:Ruin of Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Site of former Mito Shrine honden File:East view from Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View east from Mito Shrine * Also, west of shrine buildings stand a Daijingū stone monument and a stone-hokora-form Yasaka Shrine (deity: Susanoo-no-Mikoto). File:Yasaka-jinja shrine and Stone shrine of Daijingu.JPG|Precinct shrine of Mito Shrine
Yasaka Shrine and Daijingū stone monument File:Yasaka-jinja shrine at Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Precinct shrine Yasaka Shrine === Mito Shrine before the disaster === File:Haiden of Mito-jinja before Massive earthquake of 11 March,2011.jpg|Mito Shrine haiden before disaster
Provided by Mr. Mori Yukihiko, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ise Ōmikami Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Image of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, shintai of precinct Yasaka Shrine
Provided by Mr. Mori Yukihiko, Ise Ōmikami Shrine === References === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōsō|1=UNKNOWN}}shi (Sōma City History 4), {{ill|lt=Sōma|Q696456|WD=Q696456|1=Sōma, Fukushima}}, 1969 {{Reflist}} === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related item === {{Commonscat}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=福島県}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Mito Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Mito Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:福島県の神社]] [[Category:福島県の旧村社]] [[Category:陸奥国の式内社]] [[Category:行方郡 (福島県)|社みとしんしや]] [[Category:Buildings in Minamisoma, Fukushima]] [[Category:南相馬市の歴史]][[Category:qq]]

== name in kana (P1814) == * みとじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 6380005007220 == Commons category (P373) == * Mito-jinja, Minamisouma == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '福島県南相馬市鹿島区北右田釼宮112', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Mito-jinja, Minamisouma}} [[JA:御刀神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11488802}} == Japanese content == __NOTOC__ {{神社 |名称 = Mito Shrine |画像 = [[File:Haiden and Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|250px|Mito Shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=temporary shrine building|1=UNKNOWN}}]]
Mito Shrine after the earthquake |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094|1=Minamisōma}}{{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757|1=Kashima-ku}}112 Kenmiya, Kita-Migita |位置 = {{Coord|37|42|5.5|N|140|59|24.9|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Mito Shrine}} |祭神 = {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} |別名 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken-no-miya|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}} |例祭 = {{ill|lt=April 15|Q2497|WD=Q2497|1=April 15}} |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Mito Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Mito Shrine''' (みとじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757|1=Kashima-ku}}, {{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094|1=Minamisōma}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old ranking|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was village shrine. It is one of eight Engishiki-listed shrines in Namekata District{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=of Engishiki|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. === Enshrined deity === * Enshrines {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}}. ** The Sōma-domain history '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōsōshi|1=UNKNOWN}}' says Susanoo-no-Mikoto, but shrine tradition says this may be a record error confusing the deity of '''Yasaka Shrine''', a precinct shrine of Mito Shrine. === Origin === It is said that in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}, when {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} campaigned eastward, the deity was invited here. In {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}} 5 ({{ill|lt=905|Q24239|WD=Q24239|1=905}}), Mito Shrine was listed among 100 Engishiki shrines of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}} as a shrine with spiritual efficacy for protection of the state. In {{ill|lt=Kanki|Q1194200|WD=Q1194200|1=Kangi}} 1 (1229), the Namekata clan rebuilt shrine buildings and donated sacred fields; in {{ill|lt=Shōtoku|Q1193848|WD=Q1193848|lt=Shōtoku|1=Shōtoku (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1712|Q6871|WD=Q6871|1=1712}}), lord {{ill|lt=Sōma Takatane|Q8515901|WD=Q8515901|1=Sōma Takatane}} of {{ill|lt=Sōma Nakamura Domain|Q844079|WD=Q844079|1=Sōma Nakamura Domain}} donated 5 koku 3 shō. It was thus revered by both domain lords and local people. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 11 it was designated village shrine. The enshrined deity is traditionally identified as martial deity Futsunushi-no-Kami of {{ill|lt=Katori Jingū|Q372380|WD=Q372380|1=Katori Jingū}} in {{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011|1=Chiba Prefecture}}. As seen in place name "Kenmiya" and shrine name, it is a shrine of the "sword," and records say the {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532|1=Shintai}} is an "ancient sword of a thousand years." Standing near the sea amid rice fields, Mito Shrine was hit by the great tsunami of the {{ill|lt=2011|Q1994|WD=Q1994|1=2011}} {{ill|lt=March 11|Q2398|WD=Q2398|1=March 11}} {{ill|lt=Tohoku earthquake and tsunami|Q36204|WD=Q36204|1=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}. The sacred grove remained, but torii, shrine buildings, and precinct shrines were swept away, and many nearby parishioner homes were also lost. After the disaster, restoration projects were carried out with support from shrine offices of various prefectures; with backing from {{ill|lt=Hokkaidō Shrine Office|Q135249444|WD=Q135249444|1=Hokkaido Prefectural Shrine Association}} and {{ill|lt=Shitaya Shrine|Q11361339|WD=Q11361339|1=Shitaya Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}, a temporary shrine building was erected in the precinct on June 11. The "image of Susanoo-no-Mikoto," shintai of precinct Yasaka Shrine, was also found in the rubble. === Precinct === * Temporary shrine building (haiden + honden) File:Main approach to Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View from remains of Mito Shrine torii File:Precincts of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine precinct
Yasaka Shrine stands beside the Daijingū stone monument left of shrine building File:Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine building
Objects related to the shrine swept away by tsunami are gathered File:Ruin of Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Site of former Mito Shrine honden File:East view from Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View east from Mito Shrine * Also, west of shrine buildings stand a Daijingū stone monument and a stone-hokora-form Yasaka Shrine (deity: Susanoo-no-Mikoto). File:Yasaka-jinja shrine and Stone shrine of Daijingu.JPG|Precinct shrine of Mito Shrine
Yasaka Shrine and Daijingū stone monument File:Yasaka-jinja shrine at Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Precinct shrine Yasaka Shrine === Mito Shrine before the disaster === File:Haiden of Mito-jinja before Massive earthquake of 11 March,2011.jpg|Mito Shrine haiden before disaster
Provided by Mr. Mori Yukihiko, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ise Ōmikami Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Image of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, shintai of precinct Yasaka Shrine
Provided by Mr. Mori Yukihiko, Ise Ōmikami Shrine === References === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōsō|1=UNKNOWN}}shi (Sōma City History 4), {{ill|lt=Sōma|Q696456|WD=Q696456|1=Sōma, Fukushima}}, 1969 {{Reflist}} === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related item === {{Commonscat}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=福島県}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Mito Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Mito Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:福島県の神社]] [[Category:福島県の旧村社]] [[Category:陸奥国の式内社]] [[Category:行方郡 (福島県)|社みとしんしや]] [[Category:Buildings in Minamisoma, Fukushima]] [[Category:Municipal History of Minamisōma]][[Category:qq]]

== name in kana (P1814) == * みとじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 6380005007220 == Commons category (P373) == * Mito-jinja, Minamisouma == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '福島県南相馬市鹿島区北右田釼宮112', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Mito-jinja, Minamisouma}} [[JA:御刀神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11488802}} == Japanese content == __NOTOC__ {{神社 |名称 = Mito Shrine |画像 = [[File:Haiden and Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|250px|Mito Shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=temporary shrine building|1=UNKNOWN}}]]
Mito Shrine after the earthquake |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094|1=Minamisōma}}{{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757|1=Kashima-ku}}112 Kenmiya, Kita-Migita |位置 = {{Coord|37|42|5.5|N|140|59|24.9|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Mito Shrine}} |祭神 = {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} |別名 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken-no-miya|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}} |例祭 = {{ill|lt=April 15|Q2497|WD=Q2497|1=April 15}} |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Mito Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Mito Shrine''' (みとじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757|1=Kashima-ku}}, {{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094|1=Minamisōma}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old ranking|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was village shrine. It is one of eight Engishiki-listed shrines in Namekata District{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=of Engishiki|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. === Enshrined deity === * Enshrines {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}}. ** The Sōma-domain history '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōsōshi|1=UNKNOWN}}' says Susanoo-no-Mikoto, but shrine tradition says this may be a record error confusing the deity of '''Yasaka Shrine''', a precinct shrine of Mito Shrine. === Origin === It is said that in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}, when {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} campaigned eastward, the deity was invited here. In {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}} 5 ({{ill|lt=905|Q24239|WD=Q24239|1=905}}), Mito Shrine was listed among 100 Engishiki shrines of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}} as a shrine with spiritual efficacy for protection of the state. In {{ill|lt=Kanki|Q1194200|WD=Q1194200|1=Kangi}} 1 (1229), the Namekata clan rebuilt shrine buildings and donated sacred fields; in {{ill|lt=Shōtoku|Q1193848|WD=Q1193848|lt=Shōtoku|1=Shōtoku (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1712|Q6871|WD=Q6871|1=1712}}), lord {{ill|lt=Sōma Takatane|Q8515901|WD=Q8515901|1=Sōma Takatane}} of {{ill|lt=Sōma Nakamura Domain|Q844079|WD=Q844079|1=Sōma Nakamura Domain}} donated 5 koku 3 shō. It was thus revered by both domain lords and local people. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 11 it was designated village shrine. The enshrined deity is traditionally identified as martial deity Futsunushi-no-Kami of {{ill|lt=Katori Jingū|Q372380|WD=Q372380|1=Katori Jingū}} in {{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011|1=Chiba Prefecture}}. As seen in place name "Kenmiya" and shrine name, it is a shrine of the "sword," and records say the {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532|1=Shintai}} is an "ancient sword of a thousand years." Standing near the sea amid rice fields, Mito Shrine was hit by the great tsunami of the {{ill|lt=2011|Q1994|WD=Q1994|1=2011}} {{ill|lt=March 11|Q2398|WD=Q2398|1=March 11}} {{ill|lt=Tohoku earthquake and tsunami|Q36204|WD=Q36204|1=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}. The sacred grove remained, but torii, shrine buildings, and precinct shrines were swept away, and many nearby parishioner homes were also lost. After the disaster, restoration projects were carried out with support from shrine offices of various prefectures; with backing from {{ill|lt=Hokkaidō Shrine Office|Q135249444|WD=Q135249444|1=Hokkaido Prefectural Shrine Association}} and {{ill|lt=Shitaya Shrine|Q11361339|WD=Q11361339|1=Shitaya Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}, a temporary shrine building was erected in the precinct on June 11. The "image of Susanoo-no-Mikoto," shintai of precinct Yasaka Shrine, was also found in the rubble. === Precinct === * Temporary shrine building (haiden + honden) File:Main approach to Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View from remains of Mito Shrine torii File:Precincts of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine precinct
Yasaka Shrine stands beside the Daijingū stone monument left of shrine building File:Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine building
Objects related to the shrine swept away by tsunami are gathered File:Ruin of Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Site of former Mito Shrine honden File:East view from Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View east from Mito Shrine * Also, west of shrine buildings stand a Daijingū stone monument and a stone-hokora-form Yasaka Shrine (deity: Susanoo-no-Mikoto). File:Yasaka-jinja shrine and Stone shrine of Daijingu.JPG|Precinct shrine of Mito Shrine
Yasaka Shrine and Daijingū stone monument File:Yasaka-jinja shrine at Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Precinct shrine Yasaka Shrine === Mito Shrine before the disaster === File:Haiden of Mito-jinja before Massive earthquake of 11 March,2011.jpg|Mito Shrine haiden before disaster
Provided by Mr. Mori Yukihiko, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ise Ōmikami Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Image of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, shintai of precinct Yasaka Shrine
Provided by Mr. Mori Yukihiko, Ise Ōmikami Shrine === References === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōsō|1=UNKNOWN}}shi (Sōma City History 4), {{ill|lt=Sōma|Q696456|WD=Q696456|1=Sōma, Fukushima}}, 1969 {{Reflist}} === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related item === {{Commonscat}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=福島県}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Mito Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Mito Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:福島県の旧村社]] [[Category:陸奥国の式内社]] [[Category:行方郡 (福島県)|社みとしんしや]] [[Category:Buildings in Minamisoma, Fukushima]] [[Category:Municipal History of Minamisōma]][[Category:qq]]

'福島県南相馬市鹿島区北右田釼宮112', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Mito-jinja, Minamisouma}} [[JA:御刀神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11488802}} == Japanese content == __NOTOC__ {{神社 |名称 = Mito Shrine |画像 = [[File:Haiden and Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|250px|Mito Shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=temporary shrine building|1=UNKNOWN}}]]
Mito Shrine after the earthquake |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094|1=Minamisōma}}{{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757|1=Kashima-ku}}112 Kenmiya, Kita-Migita |位置 = {{Coord|37|42|5.5|N|140|59|24.9|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Mito Shrine}} |祭神 = {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} |別名 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken-no-miya|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}} |例祭 = {{ill|lt=April 15|Q2497|WD=Q2497|1=April 15}} |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Mito Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Mito Shrine''' (みとじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757|1=Kashima-ku}}, {{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094|1=Minamisōma}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old ranking|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was village shrine. It is one of eight Engishiki-listed shrines in Namekata District{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=of Engishiki|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. === Enshrined deity === * Enshrines {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}}. ** The Sōma-domain history '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōsōshi|1=UNKNOWN}}' says Susanoo-no-Mikoto, but shrine tradition says this may be a record error confusing the deity of '''Yasaka Shrine''', a precinct shrine of Mito Shrine. === Origin === It is said that in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}, when {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} campaigned eastward, the deity was invited here. In {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}} 5 ({{ill|lt=905|Q24239|WD=Q24239|1=905}}), Mito Shrine was listed among 100 Engishiki shrines of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}} as a shrine with spiritual efficacy for protection of the state. In {{ill|lt=Kanki|Q1194200|WD=Q1194200|1=Kangi}} 1 (1229), the Namekata clan rebuilt shrine buildings and donated sacred fields; in {{ill|lt=Shōtoku|Q1193848|WD=Q1193848|lt=Shōtoku|1=Shōtoku (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1712|Q6871|WD=Q6871|1=1712}}), lord {{ill|lt=Sōma Takatane|Q8515901|WD=Q8515901|1=Sōma Takatane}} of {{ill|lt=Sōma Nakamura Domain|Q844079|WD=Q844079|1=Sōma Nakamura Domain}} donated 5 koku 3 shō. It was thus revered by both domain lords and local people. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 11 it was designated village shrine. The enshrined deity is traditionally identified as martial deity Futsunushi-no-Kami of {{ill|lt=Katori Jingū|Q372380|WD=Q372380|1=Katori Jingū}} in {{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011|1=Chiba Prefecture}}. As seen in place name "Kenmiya" and shrine name, it is a shrine of the "sword," and records say the {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532|1=Shintai}} is an "ancient sword of a thousand years." Standing near the sea amid rice fields, Mito Shrine was hit by the great tsunami of the {{ill|lt=2011|Q1994|WD=Q1994|1=2011}} {{ill|lt=March 11|Q2398|WD=Q2398|1=March 11}} {{ill|lt=Tohoku earthquake and tsunami|Q36204|WD=Q36204|1=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}. The sacred grove remained, but torii, shrine buildings, and precinct shrines were swept away, and many nearby parishioner homes were also lost. After the disaster, restoration projects were carried out with support from shrine offices of various prefectures; with backing from {{ill|lt=Hokkaidō Shrine Office|Q135249444|WD=Q135249444|1=Hokkaido Prefectural Shrine Association}} and {{ill|lt=Shitaya Shrine|Q11361339|WD=Q11361339|1=Shitaya Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}, a temporary shrine building was erected in the precinct on June 11. The "image of Susanoo-no-Mikoto," shintai of precinct Yasaka Shrine, was also found in the rubble. === Precinct === * Temporary shrine building (haiden + honden) File:Main approach to Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View from remains of Mito Shrine torii File:Precincts of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine precinct
Yasaka Shrine stands beside the Daijingū stone monument left of shrine building File:Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine building
Objects related to the shrine swept away by tsunami are gathered File:Ruin of Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Site of former Mito Shrine honden File:East view from Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View east from Mito Shrine * Also, west of shrine buildings stand a Daijingū stone monument and a stone-hokora-form Yasaka Shrine (deity: Susanoo-no-Mikoto). File:Yasaka-jinja shrine and Stone shrine of Daijingu.JPG|Precinct shrine of Mito Shrine
Yasaka Shrine and Daijingū stone monument File:Yasaka-jinja shrine at Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Precinct shrine Yasaka Shrine === Mito Shrine before the disaster === File:Haiden of Mito-jinja before Massive earthquake of 11 March,2011.jpg|Mito Shrine haiden before disaster
Provided by Mr. Mori Yukihiko, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ise Ōmikami Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Image of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, shintai of precinct Yasaka Shrine

[[File:Haiden and Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|250px|Mito Shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=temporary shrine building|1=UNKNOWN}}]]
Mito Shrine after the earthquake |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094|1=Minamisōma}}{{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757|1=Kashima-ku}}112 Kenmiya, Kita-Migita |位置 = {{Coord|37|42|5.5|N|140|59|24.9|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Mito Shrine}} |祭神 = {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} |別名 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken-no-miya|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ken Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}} |例祭 = {{ill|lt=April 15|Q2497|WD=Q2497|1=April 15}} |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Fukushima|label=Mito Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Mito Shrine''' (みとじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Kashima-ku|Q24895757|WD=Q24895757|1=Kashima-ku}}, {{ill|lt=Minamisōma|Q642094|WD=Q642094|1=Minamisōma}}, {{ill|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|Q71707|WD=Q71707|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old ranking|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was village shrine. It is one of eight Engishiki-listed shrines in Namekata District{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=of Engishiki|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. === Enshrined deity === * Enshrines {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Kami|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|1=Futsunushi}}. ** The Sōma-domain history '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōsōshi|1=UNKNOWN}}' says Susanoo-no-Mikoto, but shrine tradition says this may be a record error confusing the deity of '''Yasaka Shrine''', a precinct shrine of Mito Shrine. === Origin === It is said that in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}, when {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} campaigned eastward, the deity was invited here. In {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|WD=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}} 5 ({{ill|lt=905|Q24239|WD=Q24239|1=905}}), Mito Shrine was listed among 100 Engishiki shrines of {{ill|lt=Mutsu Province|Q907389|WD=Q907389|1=Mutsu Province}} as a shrine with spiritual efficacy for protection of the state. In {{ill|lt=Kanki|Q1194200|WD=Q1194200|1=Kangi}} 1 (1229), the Namekata clan rebuilt shrine buildings and donated sacred fields; in {{ill|lt=Shōtoku|Q1193848|WD=Q1193848|lt=Shōtoku|1=Shōtoku (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1712|Q6871|WD=Q6871|1=1712}}), lord {{ill|lt=Sōma Takatane|Q8515901|WD=Q8515901|1=Sōma Takatane}} of {{ill|lt=Sōma Nakamura Domain|Q844079|WD=Q844079|1=Sōma Nakamura Domain}} donated 5 koku 3 shō. It was thus revered by both domain lords and local people. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 11 it was designated village shrine. The enshrined deity is traditionally identified as martial deity Futsunushi-no-Kami of {{ill|lt=Katori Jingū|Q372380|WD=Q372380|1=Katori Jingū}} in {{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011|1=Chiba Prefecture}}. As seen in place name "Kenmiya" and shrine name, it is a shrine of the "sword," and records say the {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532|1=Shintai}} is an "ancient sword of a thousand years." Standing near the sea amid rice fields, Mito Shrine was hit by the great tsunami of the {{ill|lt=2011|Q1994|WD=Q1994|1=2011}} {{ill|lt=March 11|Q2398|WD=Q2398|1=March 11}} {{ill|lt=Tohoku earthquake and tsunami|Q36204|WD=Q36204|1=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}. The sacred grove remained, but torii, shrine buildings, and precinct shrines were swept away, and many nearby parishioner homes were also lost. After the disaster, restoration projects were carried out with support from shrine offices of various prefectures; with backing from {{ill|lt=Hokkaidō Shrine Office|Q135249444|WD=Q135249444|1=Hokkaido Prefectural Shrine Association}} and {{ill|lt=Shitaya Shrine|Q11361339|WD=Q11361339|1=Shitaya Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}, a temporary shrine building was erected in the precinct on June 11. The "image of Susanoo-no-Mikoto," shintai of precinct Yasaka Shrine, was also found in the rubble. === Precinct === * Temporary shrine building (haiden + honden) File:Main approach to Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View from remains of Mito Shrine torii File:Precincts of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine precinct
Yasaka Shrine stands beside the Daijingū stone monument left of shrine building File:Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Mito Shrine building
Objects related to the shrine swept away by tsunami are gathered File:Ruin of Honden of Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|Site of former Mito Shrine honden File:East view from Mito-jinja shrine.JPG|View east from Mito Shrine * Also, west of shrine buildings stand a Daijingū stone monument and a stone-hokora-form Yasaka Shrine (deity: Susanoo-no-Mikoto). File:Yasaka-jinja shrine and Stone shrine of Daijingu.JPG|Precinct shrine of Mito Shrine
